audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim post-Joshua incomplete-conquest and settlement register, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for post-Joshua inquiry, Judah-Simeon alliance, Adoni-bezek mutilation, Jerusalem/Hebron/Debir traditions, Achsah's water request, Kenite settlement, forced-labor notices, and repeated non-dispossession formulas.

verses.restored: It was after death of Joshua that children of Israel asked YHWH, saying, Who shall go up for us first against Canaanite, to fight against him?
YHWH said, Judah shall go up. Look, I have given land into his hand.
Judah said to Simeon his brother, Go up with me into my lot, and let us fight against Canaanite; I also will go with you into your lot. So Simeon went with him.
Judah went up, and YHWH gave Canaanite and Perizzite into their hand; they struck them at Bezek, ten thousand men.
They found Adoni-bezek at Bezek and fought against him, and struck Canaanite and Perizzite.
Adoni-bezek fled, and they pursued after him, seized him, and cut off thumbs of his hands and big toes of his feet.

Adoni-bezek said, Seventy kings with thumbs of their hands and big toes of their feet cut off used to gather under my table; as I have done, so Divine has repaid me. They brought him to Jerusalem, and he died there.
Children of Judah fought against Jerusalem and captured it; they struck it by edge of sword and sent city into fire.
Afterward children of Judah went down to fight against Canaanite dwelling in hill country, Negev, and Shephelah.
Judah went against Canaanite dwelling in Hebron; name of Hebron formerly was Kiriath-arba. They struck Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai.
From there he went against inhabitants of Debir; name of Debir formerly was Kiriath-sepher.
Caleb said, Whoever strikes Kiriath-sepher and captures it, I will give him Achsah my daughter as wife.
Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother, captured it, and he gave him Achsah his daughter as wife.
It was, when she came, that she urged him to ask from her father a field; she dismounted from donkey, and Caleb said to her, What is for you?
She said to him, Give me a blessing, for you have given me land of Negev; give me springs of water. Caleb gave her upper springs and lower springs.
Children of Kenite, father-in-law of Moses, went up from city of palms with children of Judah into wilderness of Judah, which is in Negev of Arad; they went and dwelt with the people.
Judah went with Simeon his brother, and they struck Canaanite dwelling in Zephath and devoted it to destruction; name of city was called Hormah/Devotion.
Judah captured Gaza and its territory, Ashkelon and its territory, and Ekron and its territory.
YHWH was with Judah, and he dispossessed hill country, but he could not dispossess inhabitants of valley because they had chariots of iron.
They gave Hebron to Caleb as Moses spoke, and he dispossessed from there three sons of Anak.
But Jebusite dwelling in Jerusalem children of Benjamin did not dispossess; Jebusite dwells with children of Benjamin in Jerusalem to this day.
House of Joseph, they also went up to Bethel, and YHWH was with them.
House of Joseph caused Bethel to be spied out; name of city formerly was Luz.
Watchers saw a man going out from city, and they said to him, Show us, please, entrance of city, and we will do covenant loyalty with you.
He showed them entrance of city, and they struck city by edge of sword; but the man and all his clan they sent away.
The man went to land of Hittites and built a city and called its name Luz; that is its name to this day.

Manasseh did not dispossess Beth-shean and its towns, Taanach and its towns, inhabitants of Dor and its towns, inhabitants of Ibleam and its towns, or inhabitants of Megiddo and its towns; Canaanite was determined to dwell in that land.
It was, when Israel grew strong, that they put Canaanite to forced labor, but they did not surely dispossess them.
Ephraim did not dispossess Canaanite dwelling in Gezer; Canaanite dwelt in their midst in Gezer.
Zebulun did not dispossess inhabitants of Kitron or inhabitants of Nahalol; Canaanite dwelt in their midst and became forced labor.
Asher did not dispossess inhabitants of Acco, Sidon, Ahlab, Achzib, Helbah, Aphik, or Rehob.
Asherite dwelt in midst of Canaanite inhabitants of land, for they did not dispossess them.

Naphtali did not dispossess inhabitants of Beth-shemesh or inhabitants of Beth-anath; he dwelt in midst of Canaanite inhabitants of land, and inhabitants of Beth-shemesh and Beth-anath became forced labor for them.
Amorite pressed children of Dan into hill country, for they did not allow them to come down to valley.
Amorite was determined to dwell at Mount Heres, Aijalon, and Shaalbim; but hand of house of Joseph grew heavy, and they became forced labor.
Border of Amorite was from ascent of Akrabbim, from Sela and upward.

translation_notes: Judges begins after Joshua by revisiting conquest as incomplete, contested, and locally negotiated rather than total and settled.

Adoni-bezek interprets his mutilation as measure-for-measure repayment for mutilations he committed. The text reports retaliatory disability-making and must not normalize torture, amputation, or revenge justice.
Jerusalem traditions in Judges 1 stand in tension with Joshua's allotment and ongoing Jebusite presence notices, requiring source and compositional audit.
Achsah's water request repeats Joshua 15 and again shows that land without springs is not viable inheritance; her agency must be preserved without romanticizing marriage-as-prize patriarchy.
The Kenite note preserves non-Israelite kinship presence through Moses' in-laws and should complicate ethnic-purity readings.
Iron-chariot notices, determined Canaanite presence, and repeated non-dispossession formulas dismantle any simple total-conquest reading.
Forced-labor notices appear across multiple tribes. Survival under tribute or labor is not justice or peaceful integration.
safety_note: Do not use Judges 1 to justify mutilation, conquest, ethnic domination, forced labor, treating women as rewards, or modern territorial claims. Center the chapter's admission of incomplete control, mixed populations, Achsah's resource demand, and the need to reject revenge and coerced labor as ethical models.
